feedback.text: I strongly support the digital fairness act. I believe when I click on buy, I should own what I "buy". When I lease something, I am not buying it. I strongly feel like a lot of mechanics on websites today aim to get users to believe they purchase something when in reality all they do is pay for access to a license. I do not wish to "buy" access to a license. Yes, developers, publishers and sellers do have to earn money and I am absolutely fine with paying for products. I do however dislike that today, whenever I pay for something, I am halfway sure that the product I "buy" gets revoked someday later. I absolutely agree that products need to cost money. But if I pay for something, I want to own what I paid for. When I pay for access to a license, I should get my money back when the license ends, unless it clearly states how long my access is going to last. And even then, it should be mandatory to say "lend", "lease" or "subscribe" - or something similar - on any site where I "buy" any product for a set ( or as of today, unset) amount of time. I think that digital goods should count as a product. I also do think that those products should cost something. But I want to know what I am paying for up front. Not on page 50/67 of an EULA, license agreement or whatever the other party wants to call their terms and conditions. Terms and conditions should not be able to be changed on any purchase that includes a service. Yes, there are reasons that should form an exception from that but in general, terms and conditions on services should be binding for both parties. When I buy a refrigerator and after a year the producer decides that vegetables are bad and changes the terms so that I am no longer allowed to store vegetables in "my" refrigerator and my continued use of the device is my agreement to the updated terms, this is just unfair. Most terms and conditions contain paragraphs telling users that the producer has the right to terminate the contract for any (and sometimes even no) reason at any time. In my oppinion, this should be illegal or at least require that the purchase is refunded. I do strongly hope that one day when I buy something, I own it again. Be it digitally or otherwise. In most cases you do not own what you buy, many websites feature quick purchase forms but no return/retire/revoke forms and the only possibility you have to achieve this is by some sort of chatbot, customer agent via email/chat and in some cases unless you specifically say what you need to say, you do not get your money back. I am sad about the state in which many youtubers and other celebrities say whatever they have been told to in order to sell end users products, often misleading or even outright lieing to their viewers. Yes, by now there are headers, icons or text indicating that the current statement is an advertisement. But that does not make lies and misleading statements okay. You should be able to purchase stuff based on what is advertised and not need to research the product in order to get to know what it really can/is. And I strongly feel that when there is a dispute (for example on ebay), that the platform should not be able to just say "Oh, you didn't receive your product? Oh, how sad. But sadly it is not covered by our money back guarantee...". Yes, the platform did not sell me the item. But the platform took my money and (as far as I know) did not instantly pay out that money to the seller. So when the seller does not send me anything, I should be able to quickly get my money back. From that platform and not from my payment processor (in this case paypal) and especially not after a month of waiting for the "seller" to respond in several stages. Again, I strongly support the digital fairness act.
